coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of diazinon as an organophosphorus poison in real samples using fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Khaledian, Salar
Noroozi-Aghideh, Ali
Kahrizi, Danial
Moradi, Sajad
Abdoli, Mohadese
Ghasemalian, Akbar Haji
Heidari, Mohammad Foad
coredata.dc:description: Due to the growing population of the world and the concerns related to food security, the application of pesticides in agriculture has increased in order to increasing the production efficacy. Excessive use of such chemicals can lead to serious contaminations in environmental and food chains. In this way, it is necessary to find more accurate and fast approaches to the detection of pesticide residuos in food. In this paper, a simple, rapid and inexpensive nanosensor based on fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) has been reported for the detection of diazinon on real samples. The synthesized CDs were characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), x-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The obtained CDs showed maximum fluorescence emission at 485 nm when excited at 393 nm. Upon the addition of diazinon, the emission was quenched accordingly with a concentration-dependent manner. The designed nanosensor could specifically detect diazinon in comparison to other organophosphorus poisons. The sensor has achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.038 ± 0.01 µM and a dynamic linear range of up to 13 µM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Sensing
Quenching
Fluorescence
Organophosphorus poison

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of pH, reaction temperature and reaction time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The maximum excitation and emission spectra of CDs.
FTIR spectrum of Tomato juices (A) and CDs (B).
TEM image and SPSS size distribution histogram of the freshly prepared CDs (A and B). (The sizes of 100 particles were measured for SPSS analysis).
Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the CDs.
The effect of pH (A), reaction temperature (B) and reaction time (C) on the fluorescent intensity ratio of the diazinon-CDs system.

The quenching in fluorescence intensity in the presence of different concentrations of diazinon ranging from 0.05 to 13 µM. Inset shows the linear range on the relationship between intensity quenching and diazinon concentration.
The selectivity of the ratiometric sensor to various organophosphorus poison.
The graphical of green synthesis of CDs and quenching the FL intensity in the presence of diazinon.
Detection of diazinon using different sensing platforms.
Determination of Diazinon in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: C spiked = Concentration of diazinon added to water samples.
C found = Concentration of diazinon that can be detected by CDs.
Relative recovery = C found/ C spiked × 100.

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of diazinon as an organophosphorus poison in real samples using fluorescence carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the growing population of the world and the concerns related to food security, the application of pesticides in agriculture has increased in order to increasing the production efficacy. Excessive use of such chemicals can lead to serious contaminations in environmental and food chains. In this way, it is necessary to find more accurate and fast approaches to the detection of pesticide residuos in food. In this paper, a simple, rapid and inexpensive nanosensor based on fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) has been reported for the detection of diazinon on real samples. The synthesized CDs were characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), x-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The obtained CDs showed maximum fluorescence emission at 485 nm when excited at 393 nm. Upon the addition of diazinon, the emission was quenched accordingly with a concentration-dependent manner. The designed nanosensor could specifically detect diazinon in comparison to other organophosphorus poisons. The sensor has achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.038 ± 0.01 µM and a dynamic linear range of up to 13 µM.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), pesticides are substances or compounds used to control or kill pests that harm plants and animals [1]. Pesticides are divided into different categories according to the type of pest, including insecticides, herbicides and fungicides[2]. Chemically and structurally, pesticides are also divided into several categories much of them are the organophosphates and organochlorines[3–5]. Diazinon is one of the most important organophosphate poisons that is widely used in agriculture and non-agriculture applications[6,7]. In recent decades, due to population growth and the growing need for food, the application of organophosphate pesticides for increasing the efficiency of the plant and animal products has increased[8,9]. With the increase in pesticide use, concerns about the presence of residual poisons in the environment and the food chain had increased in the global community[10–12]. Therefore, to track the amount of poisons remaining in the environment, it is necessary to find simple, inexpensive, fast, sensitive and selective laboratory methods for monitoring these compounds[13,14]. Although classical laboratory methods such as chromatography[15], HPLC[16], capillary electrophoresis[17], and mass spectrometry[18] are effectively used to analyze toxins in contaminated samples, they also have disadvantages that limit their applications. These limitations include time-consuming sample preparation, complexity, the need for expensive equipment, and the need for a skilled technician[19,20]. There are different types of sensors that have different applications[21–23], but fluorescence-based sensors are more important due to their unique features[24,25]. In recent years, fluorescence sensing has been reported by many researchers using quantum dots to replace classical methods for toxin detection. However these classic semi-conductive materials have been developed from CdSe, as well as PbS or other heavy metals, their use is limited due to problems such as biotoxicity and high cost[26,27]. Carbon dots are a fluorescent member of the nanocarbon family that follows fullerene, carbon nanotubes and graphene. Carbon dots are widely used in various areas such as biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery and photocatalysis, due to their stable photoluminescence, high chemical durability, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and easy to operate-modification[28–30]. There are several methods to synthesize carbon dots, including laser ablation, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation, and hydrothermal carbonization[31–34]. The sources used to synthesize carbon dots are different, ranging from graphite and activated carbon to natural materials such as gelatin, silk, cellulose, lignin, fruit extracts, etc[35–41]. This study aims to use hydrothermal carbonization as a simple and inexpensive method to synthesize a high-fluorescent carbon dot from tomato fruit extract (schematic. 1). The synthesized carbon dots are then characterized by several analyses such as TEM, FTIR, XRD, etc. Finally, the resulting carbon dot is used to detection of diazinon as an organophosphorus poison in both laboratory and real samples.Scheme1.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade or the highest purity available. Alcohol ethylic 99%, Diazinon, Malathion, Profenofos, Cypermethrin, Chlorpyrifos and Ethion were purchased from Sigma Aldrich company. Tomato fruit was prepared from a local market in Kermanshah, Iran. The morphology and size of prepared nano carbon dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used for e chemical composition of the prepared nanoparticles. Fluorescence spectra were performed with a PerkinElmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction pattern was recorded using a German Bruker D8 ADVANCE XRD with a 2θ scanning mode. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired over the range of 500–4000 cm−1 with an IR Prestige-21 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

C-dots were synthesized using tomato fruit through a simple and convenient hydrothermal method. Briefly, tomato juices were mixed with 10 ml of Alcohol ethylic 99% and transferred into 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 2 h. Then the obtained mixture cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane followed by dialyzed at 4 °C with dialysis bag (MWCO: 3500 Da) for 24 h. The obtained solution was freeze-dried to form a brown solid and stored at 4C° for further study.

The detection method is based on the degree of fluorescence quenching of the CDs in the presence of a known volume of diazinon (ranging from 10 to 110 µL with an interval of 10 µL), corrected against the fluorescence intensity of the blank CDs solution. The volume and concentration of CDs were 2.5 ml and 10 µg/ml, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectrums (excited at 393 nm) were recorded from 400 to 600 nm. Other organophosphorus poisons such as Malathion, Profenofos, Cypermethrin, Chlorpyrifos and Ethion were used to investigate the specificity of carbon dots. Real sample detection was conducted by investigating the recoveries of different spiking concentrations of diazinon in distilled, tap and river water.

The synthesized CDs were characterized by fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy, FTIR, XRD, TEM and EDX. Based on the results, the synthesized CDs exhibited distinctly blue fluorescence with a peak maximum at 485 nm upon 393 nm excitation (Fig. 1 ).
Sharply visible range absorption bands at around 248 and 317 nm are found in the UV–vis spectra of CDs (Fig. 2 ).

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to analyze the functional groups on the surface of the tomato juices and as-prepared CDs in detail (Fig. 3 A and 3B). As can be seen in the Fig. 3A, most of the peaks in tomato juices are also present in the synthesized carbon dot and only slight shifts are observed in them. Specifically, in the CDs spectrum the absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations appeared at1073 cm−1 and 3375 cm−1 with C–H and C = N stretching vibrations at 2931 cm−1, 2893 cm−1 and 2411 cm−1 respectively. Similarly, C = O and C = C stretching vibrations were at 1687 cm−1 and the peak at 1446 cm−1 was associated with C–H and N–H bending vibrations as well as the peak at 1205 cm−1 for C–O bending vibrations.
XRD pattern of synthesized CDs was shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that a broad peak around 2θ = 20-30°, which suggested the C-dots had an amorphous carbon phase.

Based on the results of the TEM image, monodispersity in size was observed for the prepared CDs(Fig. 5 A). The diameter of 100 nanoparticles was evaluated using nanostructures measurement and SPSS software for appropriate statistical analysis. According to the histogram curve the obtained CDs have a size below 30 nm(Fig. 5B).

Energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) provides clear information about the elements present in the CDs. The weight and atomic ratios of C and O of the carbon dots were were found to be 51.75 : 40.19 and 61.32 : 35.75 respectively. The results showed that carbon and oxygen (in total 92%) are the main elements of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 6 ). A minor amount of potassium was also found due to the use of tomato, which contain this element.

The prepared CDs display an excitation-wavelength-dependent emission feature due to the surface state and size distribution of nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescent emission peak specifically shifted from 430 to 500 nm with the excited fluorescent wavelength, changing from 350 to 410 nm. Furthermore, prepared CDs possessed the maximum fluorescent emission intensity located at 483 nm while being excited at 393 nm, which was selected for the following experiments. The quantum yields of the as-prepared CDs were calculated to be 38%. The
quantum yield (Φ) was calculated based on the following equation:
ΦCDs = Φs × (FCDs/FS) × (AS/ACDs)
Φ: quantum yield
CDs: Carbon Dots
S: Standard (quinine sulfate)
F: Fluorescence intensity
A: Absorbance

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of pH, reaction temperature and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions for diazinon detection, such as pH, reaction temperature and reaction time, were optimized in this study (Fig. 8 ). The range for the above characters were 4–9, 15–34 °C and 1–9 min, respectively. Based on the results pH = 7 was chosen as the optimal pH for diazinon detection. Also, the optimal reaction temperature and reaction time were obtained at 23 °C and 4 min.

To address whether the proposed strategy is specific for diazinon detection or not, five other organophosphorus poison were also investigated. These chemicals including Malathion, Profenofos, Cypermethrin, Chlorpyrifos and Ethion were tested for the optimal conditions. The detection method is based on the degree of fluorescence quenching of the CDs in the presence of a known volume of mentioned poisons, corrected against the fluorescence intensity of the blank CDs solution. As can be seen in the figure, diazinon significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of CDs compared to other poisons. The result showed that our designed system specifically detect diazinon between the other organophosphorus poisons (Fig. 10 ).

Different concentrations (0.05–5 µM) of diazinon spiked in the Distilled water, Tap water and River water samples were investigated and the results are shown in Table 2 . The recoveries ranged from 96.0% to 102.0% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 4%. It reveals that this method is an accurate, repeatable and simple method for diazinon determination.

A simple, fast, inexpensive, eco-friendly and one-step hydrothermal carbonization method was employed for the synthesis of CDs from tomato fruit extract as a carbon precursor. The obtained CDs were successfully applied for sensitive and selective detection of diazinon as an organophosphorus poison. The sensing process is based on the quenching of the CDs fluorescence by certain concentrations of the diazinon. The readout signal in the

sensing system was proportional to the amount of diazinon in the range of 0.05–13 µM with a limit of detection of 0.038 µM. Since organophosphorus poisons, such as diazinon, are structurally similar to war agents, we suggested the use of this method for detection of sarin, tabun as a war agent.

Salar Khaledian: Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ali Noroozi-Aghideh: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision. Danial Kahrizi: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Sajad Moradi: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology. Mohadese Abdoli: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Akbar Haji Ghasemalian: Formal analysis, Software. Mohammad Foad Heidari: Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: US Food and Drug Administration monitoring of pesticide residues in infant foods and adult foods eaten by infants/children
J. AOAC Int.
